emergent_themes.description: Participants who love cooking but experience significant anxiety around meal planning and decision-making, leading to compulsive shopping behaviors
Cooking serving as a deliberate escape from digital overwhelm and providing mindful, screen-free time
Working for food/grocery companies but choosing competitors for personal use, suggesting strong personal preferences override professional alignment
Ordering specifically for dishes that require equipment or skills the participant lacks, rather than convenience-driven ordering

coding_summary: P020 demonstrates a fascinating paradox of food control - she takes complete ownership of household cooking and spends an hour daily in the kitchen as a 'technology-free sanctuary,' yet experiences severe decision-making anxiety that drives daily grocery shopping (250 Lidl visits last year). Her delivery usage is highly strategic and infrequent (monthly), driven by specific capability gaps (Malaysian food) rather than convenience. The transition from student to graduate has increased her cooking due to kitchen ownership and work structure, while decreasing delivery usage. She shows remarkable loyalty to Uber Eats despite working for competitor Ocado, suggesting personal preferences override professional alignment. Her experience highlights the importance of subscription simplicity (Deliveroo's £25 minimum was a major barrier) and the power of ecosystem benefits (Uber's transport integration).
